evidence_quotes.quote: Video-game publishers routinely shut down games, be they multiplayer or single player, for reasons of expenses.
This turns a one-time sale into a de-facto rental without the clarity, pricing or safeguards normally attached to subscription models.
E-book, music and software vendors can withdraw catalogue items or shutdown services that can impact fully bought items, removing or locking customers out of their own libraries.

paragraphs.text: Hello European Commission, I've taken the opportunity to comment on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act. The initiative's goal of closing gaps in consumer protection is something i hold a great interest in. I write as a long-time consumer of digital games, films, books, software and other goods to highlight one blind spot that has come to my attention in this initiative: The sudden removal or disabling of legitimately purchased digital content, coupled with legal measures that can block private or comunity preservation. The problem in practice: - Video-game publishers routinely shut down games, be they multiplayer or single player, for reasons of expenses. This leaves paying customers with unusable titles and no lawful way of keeping them running. - Streaming platforms and online stores remove films and tv-series; if no hard-copy alternative exists, access to these bought and paid for products cease to exists. - E-book, music and software vendors can withdraw catalogue items or shutdown services that can impact fully bought items, removing or locking customers out of their own libraries. These practices turn a one-time sale into a de-facto rental without the clarity, pricing or safeguards normally attached to subscription models. They erode consumer trust and contradict the EU's declared principle that citizens should "fully enjoy the opportunities of the digital transition" written in the first paragraph of the "European Digital Rights and Principles" article. A Regulatory gap: Existing rules partly address quality and conformity (Digital Content Directive 2019/770) and allow for cultural-heritage institutions to make preservation copies (Art. 6 DSM Directive). What remains is an enforceable right for ordinary consumers to retain functional access to lawfully acquired digital works when providers abandon them. I am no politician or lawmaker, so how to tackle these issues i will omit to comment on. But i feel strongly for this cause, and believe digital products to now be as essential to European culture as physical books or appliances. When providers can retroactively void purchases, consumers bear all the risk while businesses retain unilateral control. Adding preservation-and-continued-use right to the Digital Fairness Act would restore a semblance of balance, ensuring lasting value for consumers, and safeguard Europe's digital heritage.
